narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 30.50, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 44.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their program. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, actually pushing the prediction toward you passing rather than failing, which is a bright spot in your profile. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the typical student's 5.53 average—which significantly pulled the prediction toward failure by signaling weaker performance relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average of 5.71, added further weight toward the failure risk, though with less impact than your later grades. While your LSAT score of 30.50 is lower than the average 36.70, and your middle income bracket aligns with many applicants, these played smaller roles compared to your deciles and GPA. Overall, your early and mid-undergrad rankings stood out as the biggest drags against you when stacked up to typical successful students, who often rank in higher deciles. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid GPA standing above average at 3.40. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average, and if possible, pursue postgraduate courses or experiences that could improve your academic decile rankings. Building a stronger narrative around your full-time commitment and any relevant extracurriculars could also help highlight your potential. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas—keep pushing forward, as your GPA shows you have real strengths to build on.
